channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: Four first-time astronauts with only four hours of training took the first diagnostic X-rays ever captured on human beings in orbit, and radiologists on the ground judged the images clinically usable enough to catch a fracture a quarter-million miles from the neare - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Four first-time astronauts with only four hours of training took the first diagnostic X-rays ever captured on human beings in orbit, and radiologists on the ground judged the images clinically usable enough to catch a fracture a quarter-million miles from the neare  Space Daily

  3. See the first x-rays of humans taken in space  Scientific American
  4. Astronauts Have Taken The First Human X‑Rays in Space  ScienceAlert
  5. First Diagnostic X-Rays in Space Mark New Era for Astronaut Health  Radiological Society of North America | RSNA

item.title: Saturn's moon Titan runs the same weather cycle as Earth — clouds, rain, rivers and seas — except the liquid doing all the work is methane, and the bedrock underfoot is water frozen at nearly minus 180 degrees, harder than most stone on Earth - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Saturn's moon Titan runs the same weather cycle as Earth — clouds, rain, rivers and seas — except the liquid doing all the work is methane, and the bedrock underfoot is water frozen at nearly minus 180 degrees, harder than most stone on Earth  Space Daily
  2. Saturn’s Largest Moon May Hold the Resources for a Space Colony  SciTechDaily
  3. Titan holds hundreds of times more liquid hydrocarbons than every known oil and gas reserve on Earth combined, yet you couldn't light a single drop — its air is nitrogen and methane, with almost no oxygen, so a campfire is physically impossible  Space Daily
  4. Titan could be humanity’s next deep space destination  A News
  5. Titan's resources could support future deep space missions, scientists say  Mid-day

item.title: The engineers trying to save a fading Voyager had one chance to fire thrusters frozen and unused for 37 years, and when the signal finally returned they learned the impossible had worked - Space Daily
item.description:

  4. ATLANTIC SKIES: How a spacecraft launched in 1977 is sending signals beyond our solar system  PNI Atlantic News
  5. Voyager 1 launched in 1977 with onboard computers holding less memory than a single photo on a modern phone — and that 1970s machine is still running, sending data back to Earth from interstellar space  ScienceBlog.com
